coredata.dc:title: pH-Dependent surface properties of N–Cdots obtained by the hydrothermal method with multicolored emissions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

Rodrigues, Marcelo Oliveira
Sousa, Marcelo Henrique
Campos, Alex Fabiano Cortez
coredata.dc:description: This study addresses the pH-dependent surface properties of hydrophilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N–Cdots) elaborated through a hydrothermal method. The samples were obtained using p-phenylenediamine and urea (N–CdotR), and citric acid and ammonium hydroxide (N–CdotB) as precursors, respectively. The structural and morphological properties of the N-Cdots were investigated using TEM, Raman spectroscopy and FTIR. The nanoparticles of both samples exhibited a large number of hydrophilic surface groups. Potentiometric and conductometric titrations were used to determine the pKs of the N–Cdots surface groups (pK1 = 3.4 and pK2 = 6.6 for N–CdotR; pK1 = 7.6 and pK2 = 9.9 for N–CdotB). Measurements of zeta potential and hydrodynamic diameter as a function of pH indicated that the charge modulation and colloidal stability of the N–Cdots are controlled mainly by amine and carboxyl groups in N–CdotR and carboxyl and phenolic groups in N–CdotB. The optical properties of the N–Cdots were characterized using UV-Vis spectroscopy. The photoluminescence results indicated that the acid-base behavior of the surface groups promotes changes in the energy emission states, with blue- and redshifts. Moreover, the N–Cdots presented significant quantum fluorescent yield (QY = 25% for N–CdotR and QY = 30% for N–CdotB) and good photostability. The whole of these results demonstrates that pH is a key parameter to monitor the colloidal stability and optical properties of N–Cdots as well as their potential technological applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the elaboration of the N–CdotR and N–CdotB nanoparticles by the hydrothermal method (I) and subsequent separation of the soluble fraction for further investigation (II).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), individual particles analysis with interplanar distance measurement (upper right) and size distribution diagram (bottom right) for a sample of N–CdotR (A) and N–CdotB (B). Raman spectra obtained for N–CdotR (C) and N-CdotB (D). Infrared absorption spectrum with Fourier transform (FTIR) of the N–CdotR (E) e N–CdotB (F).

Potentiometric-conductometric titrations of samples N–CdotR (A) and N–CdotB (B). The intersection of the linear segments (P1 and P2) indicates the equivalence points. The three titration regions are indicated as A, B and C, along with the pK values in the buffer regions.

Zeta potential (in red) and hydrodynamic diameter (in blue) as a function of pH for samples N–CdotR (A) and N–CdotB (C). Speciation of the molar fraction of surface sites at different pH conditions along with the indication of the position of pKs for samples N–CdotR (B) and N–CdotB (D). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

UV–vis absorption spectra for N–CdotR (A) and N–CdotB (C), emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths for N–CdotR (B) and N–CdotB (D). The spectra were collected at pH 3.4 for sample N–CdotR and pH 8.6 for N–CdotB, which correspond to the pH condition obtained after the synthesis procedure.
Absorbance and emission spectra of N–CdotR at pH = 2.0, 4.0, 8.0 and 10.0.
Absorbance and emission spectra of N–CdotB at pH = 2.0, 6.0, 8.0 and 10.0.
Results of elemental analysis (CHN) for N–CdotR and N–CdotB samples.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study addresses the pH-dependent surface properties of hydrophilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N–Cdots) elaborated through a hydrothermal method. The samples were obtained using p-phenylenediamine and urea (N–CdotR), and citric acid and ammonium hydroxide (N–CdotB) as precursors, respectively. The structural and morphological properties of the N-Cdots were investigated using TEM, Raman spectroscopy and FTIR. The nanoparticles of both samples exhibited a large number of hydrophilic surface groups. Potentiometric and conductometric titrations were used to determine the pKs of the N–Cdots surface groups (pK1 = 3.4 and pK2 = 6.6 for N–CdotR; pK1 = 7.6 and pK2 = 9.9 for N–CdotB). Measurements of zeta potential and hydrodynamic diameter as a function of pH indicated that the charge modulation and colloidal stability of the N–Cdots are controlled mainly by amine and carboxyl groups in N–CdotR and carboxyl and phenolic groups in N–CdotB. The optical properties of the N–Cdots were characterized using UV-Vis spectroscopy. The photoluminescence results indicated that the acid-base behavior of the surface groups promotes changes in the energy emission states, with blue- and redshifts. Moreover, the N–Cdots presented significant quantum fluorescent yield (QY = 25% for N–CdotR and QY = 30% for N–CdotB) and good photostability. The whole of these results demonstrates that pH is a key parameter to monitor the colloidal stability and optical properties of N–Cdots as well as their potential technological applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (Cdots) are carbon nanoparticles that stand out for presenting exceptional luminescent properties compared to typical fluorophores [1]. The great potential of these nanomaterials to be applied as light-emitting devices, biomarkers and optical sensors has been attracting attention in recent years especially with regard to the understanding of their photoluminescent mechanism, which has not yet been fully elucidated [2]. Cdots, as well as luminescent inorganic semiconductor particles, exhibit a tunable fluorescent emitting center, therefore they can emit light at different wavelengths. This important property has become the driving force for the expansion of the research involving not only Cdots synthesis but also their innovative potential applications for bioimaging, photonics, optics, and energy storage [3]. Besides, their photostability, chemical non-reactivity, easy surface modifications, and low production cost endow Cdots with a broad scope of application [4–6].

The methods of Cdots synthesis can be classified as bottom-up and top-down according to the carbon source used. Top-down synthesis is carried out by exfoliating/fragmenting a bulk carbon source such as graphite, graphene, carbon fiber and coal to obtain nanometric particles, which may follow a surface treatment to be dispersed in a desirable medium [7,8]. The bottom-up approach uses small molecules or salts as precursors, which can be carbonized to form Cdots under controlled conditions of temperature and pressure [9,10]. In this method, an interesting feature is the fact that some of the chemical moieties of the precursor molecules can be found on the surface of the formed Cdots.

The use of heteroatoms (like N, S, Se, B, F elements) for doping carbon nanostructures such as Cdots has been an effective strategy for modulating their intrinsic properties allowing the tunability of their chemical and optical properties [11–13]. Among many heteroatoms, nitrogen can be considered as an ideal dopant, due to its atomic size comparable to that of carbon. Also, the doping nitrogen atoms, which are present in the graphitic nucleus and the surface groups of Cdots, can supply excess electrons to the structure [14], thus changing its electronic transport properties and optical properties with a marked decrease in the band gap and displacement for red emission [15,16].

One of the consequences of the decrease in the average particle size is the increase in the surface/volume ratio [17]. In nanometric structures, this effect is amplified such that the surface atoms play a major role in the properties of materials. In this sense, the understanding of the properties of nanoparticles implies the knowledge of surface phenomena. Cdots are no exception and their outstanding luminescent properties dramatically depend on the chemical moieties present on their surface and their interaction with the environment.

The study of pH-dependent properties is an important step towards understanding the stability of nanocolloidal systems and their applications. These properties arise mainly from the acid-base behavior of the colloidal particles, which is strongly affected by the pH of the medium. In the literature, there are not many experimental surveys addressing the characterization of acid-base properties of surface groups of Cdots. For graphene oxide [18] and carbon nanotubes [19], previous studies have used the one-pot titration methodology for the determination of the pK of surface groups. In the case of Cdots, Choudhury et al. have spectroscopically estimated the pK from pH-induced changes in the absorption and emission spectra of the carbon dots at a selected wavelength [20]. Potentiometric-conductometric titrations are a very established and recognized technique to characterize the acid-base properties of surface groups (pK determination), and the mechanism of surface charge generation in colloidal nanoparticles. These techniques have been widely used in the case of iron oxides nanoparticles [21,22], but for the investigation of carbon dots their use is quite scarce [23]. Since the surface of Cdots is rich in organic groups with different acid-base properties, the application of potentiometric conductivity titrations together with measurements of zeta potential and hydrodynamic diameter appears to be a powerful tool to understand not only their colloidal stability, but also their pH- dependent properties.

In the present survey, two types of Cdots doped with nitrogen (N-Cdots) are synthesized through the hydrothermal method. The first sample was elaborated using p-phenylenediamine and urea, while the other was obtained with citric acid and ammonium hydroxide as precursors for particle formation and surface conditioning. The structural and morphological properties of the samples are characterized using TEM, Raman spectroscopy and FTIR while the optical properties are investigated using UV-Vis spectroscopy. In order to characterize the acid-base behavior of the N-Cdots nanoparticles, potentiometric-conductometric measurements are carried out to determine the pKs of the surface groups. Also, the colloidal stability of the N-Cdots as function of pH is explored through zeta potential and hydrodynamic diameter measurements. The characteristic optical properties of the N-Cdots in different pH conditions are discussed in terms of the chemical groups on nanoparticle surface, the structural defects, and the surface states with possible formation of fluorophores. The whole of the obtained results highlights the importance of the pH-dependent surface properties of N-Cdots towards the wide range of applications of these nanomaterials.

P-phenylenediamine (C6H4(NH2)2), urea (CO(NH2)2), citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O), quinine sulfate (C40H50N4O8S) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Brazil). Nitric acid (HNO3) 65%, hydrochloric acid (HCl) 37%, sulfuric acid (H2SO4) 95–97%, ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) 28–30% and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from VETEC (Brazil). All chemical reagents were analytical grade and have been used without further purification. The solutions were prepared in ultrapure water (Type 1).

As schematized in Fig. 1, the (red-emission) N–CdotR and (blue-emission) N–CdotB nanoparticles were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal route using a slightly modified procedure described in literature [24]. Briefly, for N–CdotR 0.72 g of p-phenylenediamine and 0.2 g of urea (1.4 g of citric acid and 0.46 mL of ammonium hydroxide for N–CdotB) were dissolved in 50 mL of water and transferred to an 80 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave that was heated for 10 h at 160 ºC. After cooling, the intense violet (N–CdotR) and yellowish-brown (N–CdotB) colors of the obtained dispersions indicated the formation of carbon nanoparticles. In order to remove water-insoluble fractions, the dispersions were treated twice with a mixture of water and ethyl acetate (1:1 ratio by volume) using a separating funnel. The water-soluble fractions were dried in an oven at 80 ºC for 24 h and the obtained powders were stored for further investigations.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained in a JEM-2100F JEOL microscope (at 200 kV). The elemental CHN analysis was performed on a Perkin Elmer combustion analyzer model EA 2400 Serie II. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a spectrophotometer Shimadzu model IRPrestige-21. Raman spectra were obtained with a confocal microscope model LAbRAM HR Evolution Horiba coupled to a CCD (Charge Coupled Device), using a green semiconductor diode laser with a grid of 18000 line/mm. The ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectra were measured in a Hitachi UV–vis spectrophotometer model U-3900H and the fluorescence spectra were measured on a Hitachi fluorescence spectrometer model F-7000. The quantum yield (QY) of the samples was calculated as described in literature [25,26] and detailed in the Supplementary Material (SM). To evaluate the optical properties as a function of pH, the absorption and emission of samples (10 mg/L) at different excitation lengths were measured. The pH of N-CdotR (2.0; 4.0; 8.0 and 10.0) and N-CdotR (2.0; 6.0; 8.0 and 10.0) dispersions were adjusted using NaOH and HNO3 solutions. The zeta potential (ζ) and hydrodynamic diameter (dH) of the N-Cdots were determined as a function of pH on a NanoZS particle analyzer using a flow cell (DTS1070) and an automatic MPT-2 Autotitrator (Malvern Instruments) with NaOH and/or HNO3 standard solutions as titrants. Solutions of each sample at 10 mg/L were used and all measurements were performed in triplicate. Previously to the titrations, the N-CdotR dispersion was alkalinized (NaOH) and the N-CdotB dispersion was acidified (HNO3). The obtained electrophoretic mobility values were converted to zeta potential using the Smoluchowski approximation [27,28] and the hydrodynamic diameter values were calculated by means of the Stokes-Einstein equation [27].

The simultaneous potentiometric-conductometric titrations for determining the pKs of the N-Cdots were performed using a Brand Titrette® Class A precision semi-automatic digital burette. The pH readings were done in a Metrohm 827 pH Lab meter with Metrohm double junction electrode (Ref. 6.0258.600), while the conductivity readings were carried out using a Metrohm 900 Touch Control of operation connected to a Metrohm 856 Conductivity Module using a Metrohm conductivity cell with constant 0.62 cm−1 (Ref. 6.0915.100) and a Metrohm 801 Magnetic Stirrer. For the titrations, 50 mL of each N-Cdot dispersion at 50 mg/L were used. Previously, the N-CdotR solution was alkalinized with NaOH until pH ~12.0 and then titrated with a 0.1 mol/L HNO3 until pH ~2.0. The N-CdotB solution was acidified with HNO3 to pH ~2.0 and then titrated with a 0.1 mol/L NaOH until pH ~12. During the titration, the titrant volume, the pH and the conductivity of the solution were measured simultaneously in the same container under CO2-free atmosphere. Blank solutions of p-phenylenediamine and citric acid, used in the synthesis of N-CdotR and N-CdotB, respectively, were titrated under the same conditions as the nanoparticles. All the experiments were performed in triplicate.

Table 1 lists the percentage of C, H, and N obtained from elemental analysis. N–CdotR and N–CdotB present a high amount of carbon, 64.3% and 61.0%, respectively, which is concentrated in the core [29]. Also, a relatively high percentage of nitrogen was found in both samples (24.1% in N–CdotR and 21.3% in N–CdotB). The higher value for sample N-CdotR is expected since both precursors used in its synthesis (P-phenylenediamine and Urea) have nitrogen in their composition. Moreover, nitrogen attached to aromatic rings favors the presence of nitrogen in the graphitic structure of the NPs core [15]. In the case of N–CdotB, the nitrogen in NPs arises from NH4 + ions of the precursor ammonium hydroxide, as reported by Wang et al. [30]. The oxygen content presents significant difference between samples (5.4% for N–CdotR and 11.4% for N–CdotB), which can be related to the composition of the precursors.

Fig. 2A and B exhibit the TEM images of N-CdotR and N-CdotB samples, respectively, indicating that the NPs of both samples are nearly spherical-shaped. Moreover, the high-resolution TEM images of individual NPs shown in the upper insets of Fig. 2A and B reveal the graphitic structure of the N-Cdots carbon core. The interplanar distances of 0.20 nm (N-CdotR) and 0.13 nm (N-CdotB), shown in the lattice fringes, correspond respectively to the (100) and (110) lattice spacing of sp2 graphitic carbon, as also observed by Holá et al. [15] and Ding et al. [24]. The lower insets of Fig. 2A and B shows the size histograms, which were obtained from the random counting of ~ 200 particles and well fitted by a log-normal distribution function. The mean size of samples N-CdotR and N-CdotB was 3.3 nm (s = 0.36) and 1.7 nm (s = 0.21), respectively.

Raman characterization corroborates TEM and elemental analysis. The Raman spectrum of N-CdotR (Fig. 2C) reveals a D band located at 1400 cm−1 that corresponds to a disordered structure and originates from defects activated in sp2 hybridized carbons. Also, a G band located at 1600 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration mode E2 g in the plane. The signals located at 1525 and 1542 cm−1 are characteristic of aromatic carbon and C—N present in the structure of N-CdotR [31,32]. Besides, the ratio of the band intensities (ID/IG) can be used to estimate the degree of crystallinity and the relative abundance of carbon in the core/surface [1]. The ID/IG is ~0.89 for N–CdotR, suggesting a greater amount of graphitic carbon in the core (ID < IG). For the N–CdotB (Fig. 2D) the D band is located at 1385 cm−1 and the G band at 1600 cm−1. The ID/IG is ~0.93, which also indicates a greater amount of graphitic carbon in the structure.

FTIR allowed us prospecting the main functional groups that could be present on the surface of N–Cdots. The FTIR spectrum of N-CdotR in Fig. 2E shows stretching vibrations due to O—H (centered at 3445 cm−1), N—H (from -NH2 groups, between 3335 and 3219 cm−1) and C—O (from -COOH groups, at 1283 cm−1). Jiang et al. [5] found that these moieties arise from the synthesis precursors and remain on the surface of the N-Cdots after hydrothermal synthesis. In a synthesis applying the same precursors and similar reagent ratios as used in this work, Ding et al. [24] found a proportion of superficial sites [-NH2]/[ -COOH] ~3 in their obtained carbon dots. Thus, one infers that the surface of N-CdotR is functionalized largely by amino groups (-NH2) with a smaller portion of carboxyl (-COOH) moieties, as schematized in Fig. 1. These hydrophilic groups are the main responsible for the high dispersibility of NPs in water, as experimentally observed. Besides, stretching vibrations C˭C at 1511 cm−1, C˭N at 1617 cm−1, and -C—N = at 1425 cm−1 were observed, indicating the formation of polyaromatic structures in the core of NPs [24,33,34]. It is well known that urea under high-temperature conditions can form cyanuric acid and cyclic structures like ammelide and melamine [35,36], which have double and simple bonds between nitrogen and carbon in cyclic structures.

The infrared spectrum of N–CdotB (Fig. 2F) shows an intense band at 3050–3650 cm−1 mainly due to the vibrations of the O—H. Also, a strong absorption peak at 1712 cm−1 can be ascribed to C˭O stretching vibration of α, β unsaturated carboxylic acid and carbonyls at 1600 cm−1 [30]. Amide (in the range 1350–1000 cm−1), C˭N vibration at 1660 cm−1, C—N vibration at 1357 cm−1 and a folding vibration between 800 and 500 cm−1 characteristics of amines [32] are also observed. Moreover, C—O at 1282 cm−1 and C˭C at 1574 cm−1 bands can also be found in the spectra. As reported by Kasprzyk et al. [37] and Schneider et al. [38], citric acid initiates a dehydration process in the presence of NH3 and derivatives at high temperatures generating pyridine ring-based fluorophores species such as 4-hydroxy-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine-1,2,6(2H,5H)-trione (HPPT), 5-oxo-1,2,3,5-tetrahydroimidazo[1,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (IPCA), and citrazinic acid. Particularly IPCA and citrazinic acid present -COOH, pyridine ring, and ketone and/or hydroxyl groups in terminal positions. Thus, N-CdotB sample presents the predominance of -COOH and -OH groups on the surface, as schematized in Fig. 1. This also corroborates the higher water solubility of N-CdotB when compared to N-CdotR.

Even though the FTIR results allowed us to gain further structural insights about the surface groups of the N-Cdots, the simultaneous potentiometric-conductometric titrations are important to characterize their acid-base behavior and the surface charge of the nanoparticles.

The potentiometric-conductometric titration curves for sample N–CdotR are shown in Fig. 3A, where two equivalent points named P1 and P2 were obtained using the linear segment extrapolation technique [39]. These points delimit three regions named A, B, and C on the titration curve. Region A corresponds to the titration of the free OH- ions from the strong base used to adjust the pH of the medium. The conductivity decreases gradually due to the neutralization of OH- ions and their replacement by NO3 - ions from the titrant, which have lower molar conductivity [40]. Also, the potentiometric curve in this region shows a mono-log behavior characteristic of the titration of a strong base. After P1, we have a region B, which corresponds to the titration of the N-CdotR surface groups, related to the equilibrium -NH2
↔ -NH3 + in region B1 and the equilibrium -COO-
↔ -COOH in region B2. The sharp increase of conductivity is related to the increase of the number moles of NO3 - ions from the titrant. The contribution of N-CdotR to conductivity is negligible due to the mass of the particles. The potentiometric curve in region B shows a characteristic profile of a weak diprotic base [41] with two buffer regions, through the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation, the pKs are found in these buffer regions, corresponding to pK2 = 6.6 and pK1 = 3.4 of the acid-base balance of the amine and carboxyl sites, respectively. As previously mentioned in Section 3.1, the number of -NH2 sites is greater than that of -COOH sites and this difference is noticeable in the titration curve when observing the volume of titrant necessary to promote protonation in each type of site. In this case, the volume required to titrate the amine sites are approximately three times larger than for carboxylic sites. After P2, region C is related to the titrant excess, which strongly increases conductivity due to the high molar conductivity of the free H3O+ ions.

The potentiometric-conductometric titration curve for sample N-CdotB is shown in Fig. 3B. The equivalence points P1 and P2 are determined as previously described for the sample N-CdotR and similar three regions are delimited A, B and C. In region A, the free H3O+ ions from the strong acid used to adjust the pH are titrated and the decrease in conductivity indicates the gradual replacement of these ions by Na+ ions of the titrant solution, which have lower molar conductivity [41]. The potentiometric curve presents a mono-log behavior characteristic of the titration of a strong acid. After P1, we have a region B, in which the surface groups of sample N–CdotB are titrated, corresponding to the equilibria -COOH ↔ -COO- (carboxylic groups) and -OH ↔ -O- (phenolic groups), as highlighted in Section 3.2, fluorophores may form on the surface, which have a molar ratio between the carboxylic and hydroxyl groups of 1:1 [38]. In this region, the small increase in conductivity is due to the increase in the Na+ ions within the dispersion. As in the case of sample N-CdotR, the contribution of N-CdotB NPs to the conductivity can be neglected due to the particle mass. In addition, in region B, the potentiometric curve shows two behaviors, one sharper as the titrant is added (1.2 → 2.2 mL of titrant) and the other less sharp (2.2 → 5.4 mL of titrant), which leads us to infer that N-CdotB presents a characteristic behavior of weak diprotic acid where the pKs of the NPs surface groups have close values. Using the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation, in region B1 and B2, pK1 = 7.6 and pK2 = 9.9 are obtained, corresponding to the carboxylic and phenolic groups, respectively. Sharmistha et al. [20] found pK1 = 7.9 and pK2 = 9.5 for Cdots obtained from lemon juice, after analyzing the optical density under different pH. After P2, region C is related to the excess of titrant reagent, which strongly increases the conductivity due to the high molar conductivity of OH- ions.

The precursors used to obtain the N–Cdots were previously titrated, and the corresponding curves can be seen in Fig. S1 A (citric acid) and B (p-phenylenediamine) of SM. Table S1 of SM lists the calculated pK values, which are in good agreement with those published in the literature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zeta potential and hydrodynamic diameter determination
ce:section.ce:para: The results of zeta potential and hydrodynamic diameter as a function of pH for sample N-CdotR are shown in Fig. 4A. In highly acidic pH, the amino-protonated surface sites (-NH3 +) are responsible for the overall positive surface charge of the NPs. At pH = 2.3, the zeta potential equals to + 43,55 mV, which ensures high colloidal stability due to electrostatic repulsion between NPs [42]. The N-Cdots dispersion corresponds macroscopically to a stable sol and no phase separation is observed. According to the molar fraction of the surface sites for N-CdotR (Fig. 4B), at this pH, the -COOH sites are mostly protonated therefore do not contribute to electrostatic repulsion. Also, in this pH region, the values of hydrodynamic diameter are around 1300 nm, indicating the presence of large clusters or aggregates in the N-Cdots dispersion. However, the aggregates do not sediment due to the high average value of zeta potential. As the pH approaches pK1, the -COOH sites are gradually deprotonated giving rise to a negative charge from the -COO- sites. The zeta potential then decreases because of the net balance of positive and negative charges on surface. It leads to lower repulsion between particles increasing the tendency of agglomeration, which can be observed in the increase of the hydrodynamic diameter for 3.0 < pH < 4.5. For pH ≥ 5.0, the carboxylic sites are completely deprotonated and begin to exert an influence on the electrostatic repulsion between the particles. Even though the overall charge decreases, as indicated by the zeta potential, the hydrodynamic diameter gradually decreases. Approaching pK2, the number of -NH3 + sites decreases due to its deprotonation to -NH2, reducing their influence on the overall surface charge. At pH = 7.2, it can be found an isoelectric point, where the nanoparticles carry no net electrical charge considering the balance of charged surface groups, and counter/co-ions present in the electric double layer up to the shear plane [43]. The value of the isoelectric point can be correlated to the molar speciation diagram (Fig. 4B), which shows that the concentration of -NH3 + and -COO- groups matches the same value at pH = 6.8 leading to a zero net proton surface charge. Thus, in this pH region, the decrease of the surface charge also decreases the repulsive interaction between particles leading to greater fluctuations of the hydrodynamic diameter (Fig. 4A) and progressive sedimentation of N-Cdots. For pH > 7.2, the surface charge inversion occurs, and the zeta potential has a negative value from the ≡COO- sites, which become the majority on the surface. It leads to a sharp decrease of the hydrodynamic diameter in the pH range of 7.0–9.0 due to the increase of the electrostatic repulsion between NPs. As a result, the N–Cdots become gradually resuspended. For pH ≥ 10.0, the charge saturation on the surface is reached, where the sites are exclusively ≡COO- leading to zeta potential values in the range of − 27.15 and − 21.30 mV and good colloidal stability. The decrease of the zeta potential modulus for higher pH values is a consequence of the high ionic strength, which screens the surface charge [44].

Fig. 4C shows the results of the zeta potential and the hydrodynamic diameter as a function of pH for N-CdotB. As previously emphasized, the surface of this Cdot sample presents mainly carboxylic (-COOH) and phenolic (-OH) groups, which can be deprotonated with increasing pH. Other chemical moieties such as pyridines and ketones exist in lower amount and do not show significant changes with pH. According to the speciation diagram of the molar fraction of surface sites shown in Fig. 4D, both the carboxylic and phenolic groups are protonated in strong acidic medium (pH < 4.0). However, the nanoparticles present a constant negative zeta potential around − 26 mV. This negative surface charge arises from the aromaticity of heterocycles, where the electronegativity of the oxygen atoms from ketones bonded to these cycles is responsible for the inductive effect on π electrons, leading to a negative charge on oxygen atoms [45]. This effect is also commonly observed in pyridine and pyrrole rings, where the non-bonding electron pair of the nitrogen atoms can be delocalized due to the inductive effect promoted by the neighbors oxygen atoms [46]. This effect may justify the negative charge of N-CdotB in this pH range, where the carboxylic and phenolic groups are protonated. The observed zeta potentials are sufficient high to avoid sedimentation, and a stable sol of N-Cdots is observed. As the pH approaches pK1 the number of -COO- sites gradually increases (Fig. 4D), leading to more negative zeta potential values. Accordingly, the hydrodynamic diameter presents a sharp reduction in the pH range of 5.0–7.0 due to the higher electrostatic repulsion between nanoparticles. As the pH continues to increase, the hydrodynamic diameter values slightly fluctuate in the range of 7.0–11.0. Also, there is an increase of the number of phenolate surface groups (≡O-), although the zeta potential values remain almost constant. For pH ≥ 11.0, NPs tend to charge saturation and the zeta potential reaches its maximum absolute value at pH 11.8, while the hydrodynamic diameter reaches its lowest value as a result of the large repulsion between nanoparticles.

Fig. S2 exhibits the hydrodynamic diameter as a function of zeta potential for both samples and the corresponding colloidal stability domains, based on macroscopic observations. As it can be seen, the key parameter to ensure the colloidal stability of the system is the zeta potential of the N–Cdots, which is tuned by the pH of the medium. As a general trend, the higher the absolute value of zeta potential, the lower the hydrodynamic size, therefore the better the colloidal stability. In the case of sample N–CdotR, stable suspensions of N–Cdots were observed in pH regions where ∣ζ∣≥ 10 mV while phase separation due to sedimentation of NPs was detected in the pH range close to the isoelectric point, where the ζ tends to zero. For sample N–CdotB, the NPs always exhibit high negative zeta potential values, which provides stable suspensions of N-Cdots in the entire investigated pH range.

Fig. 5A and B shows the absorbance and photoluminescence (PL) for N–CdotR, respectively. UV–vis absorption indicates two bands in the ultraviolet region at 249 nm and 275 nm, corresponding to the π →π* transitions (Homo → Lumo) of the aromatic rings present in the NPs core, which typically do not emit fluorescence [24]. In the region of lowest energy, four different absorption bands are observed at 300 nm, 380 nm, 432 nm, and 517 nm indicating that the sample has chemical groups that support the surface state. These bands can be assigned to the electronic n–π* transitions of chemical groups with C = O, C—OH, and C = N bonds on the surface of the N–Cdots. The absorption of these surface states is responsible for emissions at greater wavelengths [47]. The photoluminescent spectrum indicates that the emission is dependent on the excitation length varying from 475 nm to 825 nm, which can be related to the size distribution of nanoparticles and the distribution of different surface states. Table S2 compares the excitation and emission lengths observed for both samples. The data reinforce the existence of multiple states that originate from the chemical groups anchored on the surface of the N-CdotR and that the redshift is related the oxidized groups and amine groups such as alkylamines end arylamines [48].

For sample N–CdotB, the absorbance spectrum (Fig. 5C) indicates two bands in the ultraviolet region at 234 nm and 334 that can be attributed to the π→π*transitions of sp2-hybridized carbon and n→π* transitions at the edge of the carbon lattice, respectively. This absorbance profile is the same observed by Schneider et al. [38] in Cdots obtained by hydrothermal route with citric acid and ethylenediamine. The authors report that such behavior is related to electronic transitions in molecular fluorophores, formed during the synthesis procedure. At longer wavelengths, between 500 and 650 nm, N–CdotB shows a characteristic broad band. This absorption is commonly assigned to surface states related to functional groups anchored in the surface, which form a low-energy sub-band within the n–π* bandgap. The luminescent spectrum, shown in Fig. 5D, indicates a subtle emission dependence with the excitation length and a smaller number of surface states when compared to N–CdotR (Table S2). It indicates that the luminescent mechanism probably originates from both organic fluorophores and different functional groups connected to the surface and/or from surface defects [23]. As observed in the literature, there is a discrete redshift that can be attributed to the presence of intrinsic states and molecules originated from C = O and C = N bonds [23,24].

For the quantum yield calculation, the absorbance at the excitation wavelength vs integral of the emission intensity for each concentration was plotted, and a linear regression was applied to obtain the slope of the curve for N–CdotR, N–CdotB and Quinine Sulphate (Fig. S3). The fittings exhibited high correlation (R2 > 0.9) with a linear coefficient of approximately to zero for zero absorbance. The quantum yield values were 25% for N–CdotR and 30% for N-CdotB. The quantum yield presented by N–CdotB shows an almost molecular mechanism [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-dependent optical properties of the N–Cdots
ce:section.ce:para: In the literature, several authors have reported that the optical properties of the carbon dots are highly pH-dependent, where their luminescence profile is modified due to displacements in the emission length and intensity [50,51]. On the other hand, depending on the chemical composition, some carbon dots have their optical properties unchanged for different pH conditions [52,53]. As previously mentioned, the fluorescence of carbon dots can arise from the surface functional groups or the isolated fluorophores connected to surface [53]. In this context, the pH-dependence of the optical properties of the prepared N-Cdots arises from the protonation/deprotonation of the surface functional groups or from the isolated fluorophores with different energy states in acidic and basic media [48,54].

The absorbance and photoluminescence obtained at different excitation lengths for N–CdotR at pH = 2.0, 4.0, 6.0, and 10.0 are presented in Fig. 6. In pH = 2.0 the amine and carboxylic surface groups of N–CdotR are mostly protonated, and the absorption peaks are the same reported in Section 3.4, where the most intense bands occur at wavelengths between 300 and 500 nm and 500–600. With regard to the emission spectrum, peaks highlighted at 412 nm, 437 nm and 500 nm for N–CdotR can be attributed to nitrogen functional groups that favor multiple electronic states. Indeed, it has been shown that the amino or hydrazine surface groups can cause wavelength changes from the blue to green regions [48,55] and that a greater amount of nitrogen in the graphitic form in the core of the carbon dot can promote a redshift in the spectrum emission [15]. The band at 550 nm is characteristic of the carboxylic groups in their protonated form [56,57]. However, this band is displaced to 572 nm, which can be attributed to the presence of carboxylic acids in the aromatic form. It is known that protonated aromatic carboxylic acids can also causes a redshift in the fluorescence [53]. At pH = 4.0, both the absorbance and photoluminescence profiles are modified. The absorbance at wavelengths greater than 500–600 nm decreases, which can be attributed to the reduction in the multiple states present on the surface caused by proton transfer. At this pH, the carboxylic sites are mostly deprotonated (-COO-) while the amine sites remain protonated (-NH3 +) (Fig. S4). In the emission spectrum, peaks at 412 nm and 437 nm are preserved despite the pH increase, which corroborates the fact that they are associated to nitrogen in graphitic core. Also, a shift towards blue emission peaks centered at 500 nm is observed, which can be attributed to the beginning of deprotonation of -NH3 + groups [53]. At pH = 8.0, a decrease of the intensity of all bands in the absorption spectrum is observed. At this pH, the carboxylic sites and most of the amine sites are in the -COO- and -NH2 forms, respectively (Fig. S4). The emission spectra present some important modifications. For 360 nm excitation, new emission peaks at 411 nm and 469 nm are observed. Also, for excitation at longer wavelengths, the emission peaks undergo a redshift accompanied by a gradual decrease in intensity, except for excitation at 400 nm, which stands out with the highest emission intensity at 449 nm. This general decrease between energy levels was assessed by Sung et al. [48] when studying the effect of functional groups on the fluorescence of quantum dots of graphene (GQDs). The authors hypothesized that electrons not shared by amines may increase electronic density and promote a decrease in the energy difference when bonded to benzene rings. This phenomenon affects the size of the energy gap between the HOMO-LUMO orbitals, which can cause redshift. In the case of sample N–CdotR, it is reinforced by the presence of -NH2 groups on surface, which are the most abundant ones at this pH. This behavior is also observed in the emission spectra at pH = 10.0, where no changes of the configuration of surface groups are expected.

As discussed in Section 3.4, the optical characteristics of N–CdotB indicate that organic fluorophores are formed during the synthesis process, which is reinforced by the absorption bands at 234 nm (π–π * transitions) and 334 nm (n–π * transitions). These bands are also reported as indicative of the formation of organic fluorophores during the synthesis of carbon dots based on citric acid [38]. Moreover, the high quantum yield leads us to believe that the mechanism of fluorescent emission is quasi-molecular origin.

Fig. 7 shows the absorbance and photoluminescence spectra for N–CdotB at pH = 2.0, 6.0, 8.0 and 10.0. At pH = 2.0 the carboxylic acid and phenolic sites are mostly protonated, and the absorption has the same characteristics highlighted for sample N-CdotR. However, a band at 277 nm (π–π * transition) becomes prominent and the broad band located between 350 nm and 450 nm, related to the surface groups, presents greater intensity. At this pH, the emission spectra in different excitation lengths are marked by peaks at 465 nm, 483 nm and between 501 and 523 nm. These characteristic blue emission peaks may be attributed to the presence of hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH) and epoxy (-O-) functional groups and to small adjacent sp2 fragments in benzene rings, as already observed for graphene oxides (GO) [58]. The peaks between 501 and 523 nm may be associated with the energy states of the -COOH surface sites while the emissions in shorter wavelengths are related to the -OH surface sites, where both are linked to aromatic structures. It is worthy to mention that even at this pH deprotonation may occur through a intermolecular proton transfer between adjacent carboxylic and phenolic groups [53]. With pH increasing to 6.0, the carboxylic sites start to be deprotonated leading to surface changes. As a result, the absorption bands become less evident and overlap. However, there is neither displacement nor a significant decrease in intensity. The emission peaks move to 444 nm, 450 nm, 456 nm and 503 nm characterizing a blue shift. Peaks in the 517–543 nm region exhibit a redshift, which reinforces the intermolecular proton transfer between the sites. At pH = 8.0, the absorption spectrum clearly shows the bands already reported for pH = 2.0. In the case of the emission spectra, the peaks at 444 nm and 447 nm show an increase in intensity while the other peaks are preserved. At pH = 10.0, the carboxylic sites are mostly deprotonated (-COO-) and the presence of phenolate sites (-O-) becomes significant (Fig. S4). The absorption bands of shorter wavelengths overlap forming a single peak at 243 nm. At longer wavelengths, the absorption is very low, resulting from the intermolecular transfer of protons between the sites -OH and -COO-. The spectrum of photoluminescent emission exhibits a shift to blue with peaks between 441 nm and 458 nm, characteristic of the presence of phenolate groups that can form aromatic ketone (quinone formation) [59].

The above photoluminescence results of the samples in different pH conditions clearly show that the acidic medium favors the redshift mainly due to the protonated form of the aromatic carboxylic groups in both samples. As the pH increases, the deprotonation of these groups promotes a blueshift. Also, a sharp decrease in the photoluminescent intensity is observed at a strongly alkaline pH. In the case of sample N–CdotR, the persistent luminescence at larger wavelengths, even in high pH conditions, can be attributed to the higher amount of nitrogen present in the core and on the surface of the Cdots.

The present survey focused on the pH-dependent surface properties of nitrogen doped carbon dots synthesized through the hydrothermal route. The structure of the elaborated N-Cdots and their surface characteristics showed a strong dependence on the synthesis precursors, where the functional groups exerted a significant influence on both colloidal and photoluminescent properties. Simultaneous potentiometric and conductometric titrations proved to be a useful tool to determine the pKs of the chemical groups present on the N-Cdots surface. Along with the zeta potential and hydrodynamic diameter measurements, as a function of pH, the whole of the results allowed a general description of the colloidal stability mechanism of these systems, which is little explored in the literature. The photoluminescence emission behavior of the elaborated N-Cdots was also affected by the pH of the medium, due to the protonation/deprotonation and new electron distributions in the surface groups. Finally, the experimental findings in this work highlight that the understanding of the pH-dependent properties of N-Cdots is crucial for further application of these nanomaterials.
